Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell is tying the fate of $2,000 stimulus checks to the repeal of Section 230, an effort that will likely doom both pieces of legislation in the Senate.
 McConnell on Tuesday teed up a bill to increase government stimulus checks from $600 to $2,000 and repeal Section 230. Democrats have emphasized that including any provision about Section 230 would be a non-starter and amount to a "poison pill" that makes passing the $2,000 checks impossible.
 Even top Republican senators sounded skeptical of the effort on Tuesday. GOP Sen. Susan Collins, who is typically a swing vote, told Protocol she believes Section 230 should be "reformed" but does not support repeal and does not believe it should be tied to the prospect of stimulus checks.
 And Sen. John Cornyn, a high-ranking Republican with a close relationship with McConnell, said he doesn't believe Section 230 is "germane" to the COVID relief issues at hand.
  Trump over the weekend demanded that the Senate pass legislation to increase payments to Americans to $2,000 while repealing Section 230. He said that was part of the deal he struck with congressional leaders in order for him to sign the $2.3 trillion COVID relief and government spending package.
 McConnell acknowledged Trump's request on Tuesday, saying he requested to have the issues "linked." He promised that the Senate would "begin a process to bring these priorities into focus" this week.
 Aides and lobbyists told Protocol that it's almost impossible that McConnell has secured enough votes in the Senate to pass legislation that would increase checks to $2,000 and repeal Section 230. It appears to be a political move that allows McConnell to appease some of the president's top priorities without passing difficult legislation, they said.
 Even some of the senators most intent on reforming the bedrock internet law said this legislative effort has little to do with tech companies and their legal protections.
 "The Section 230 thing is not on the level," said Democratic Sen. Brian Schatz, who has introduced legislation to tweak the controversial law. "The fact that a repeal would harm conservatives, conservative websites, and Trump himself is a fine thing to point out. But I worry we are racing past the threshold question of whether this is a serious lawmaking effort. It is not."
 "I have a bipartisan bill that simply updates the law in practical ways, but the reason no one is talking about it is BECAUSE it is a serious attempt at making a law," he said.
title: 参议院多数党领袖米奇·麦康奈尔（Mitch McConnell）将2,000美元的刺激性检查的命运与第230条的废除联系在一起
title_en: Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell is tying the fate of $2,000 stimulus checks to the repeal of Section 230

     根据几则新闻报道，联邦调查局正在调查是否可能的纳什维尔自杀式炸弹袭击者安东尼·奎因·华纳（Anthony Quinn Warner）是否因与5G相关的政府监视而产生偏执，将目标对准附近的AT＆amp; T大楼。
 如果是这样的话，这将使反5G的阴谋胡言乱语达到一个新的甚至更具破坏性的水平。几个月来，白痴一直以YouTube所担心的5G技术有害于他们的健康为目标，甚至将他们假定为5G塔的目标（即使它们不是）。它甚至到达了我在纽约市附近；在我公寓对面的街道上，涂鸦上写着“ 5G杀死了生命”。
 让我清楚一点：5G不会危害您的健康，并且不会以与您当前智能手​​机中的4G技术不同的方式用于监视您。如果您在过去十年中花了任何时间看警察程序，您就会知道执法部门可以得到逮捕令，以跟踪您基于电话的位置历史记录或查看您给谁打电话或发短信。 《纽约时报》展示了如何甚至可以使用匿名位置数据通过Wi-Fi，3G或4G跟踪您。 5G并没有什么新鲜之处。
 反5G运动的大部分是社交媒体推动的恶意行为。但是，华纳的5G监控恐惧（如果属实）打开了一个新的战线，显示出5G技术，5G辩论和5G消息传递在美国的真正劣势。
    与健康相关的5G阴谋通常是基于这样的想法，即5G是未经测试的技术，需要新的基础架构，并且与诸如杀伤人员武器等危险技术有关。在我看来，它们建立在假科学家试图摆脱恐惧来赚钱或发展事业的基础上，并可能建立在试图损害竞争对手的国际坏蛋身上。
 这些阴谋理论在很多层面上都不是正确的。但是我认为无线运营商在抵消这些想法方面做得如此差劲的一个原因是，他们害怕透露自己的5G系统到底有多平凡。 
如果事先提出要求，他们将诚实：＆quot;全国范围＆quot; 5G现在提供与4G相同的性能...因为它仍然依赖于4G，以相同的频率运行，并使用许多相同的技术。但是他们不会将其放在广告牌上或推广给投资者。那将不会从苹果或三星那里出售最新的手机，也不会得到运营商的支持。订户升级其服务计划。
 对于大多数美国人而言，目前的5G几乎与4G完全一样，这是反对阴谋论的最有力论据。但是公司不能这么说，因为这会破坏他们的营销。他们必须在他们说＆＃34;时承认这一点。 5G才真正实现，＆＃34;他们跳了枪。
 与此同时，在政治层面上，5G已成为一个不连贯的热键，被特朗普总统用作一个难以理解的流行词，并被国会抛弃，成为与中国进行无形技术竞赛的图腾。
  我相信5G技术的前景。我已阅读规格。如果实施得当，在广泛的生态系统支持下，它可以履行其承诺的一切。但是，尽管有广告牌和总统推文，它还没有做任何事情。这样一来，扭曲的头脑就打开了华纳可能认为的偏执狂的大门。
   无线运营商和科技公司都在说5G是一种巨大的，改变世界的技术，现在已经可以使用，但是很少有人能从中看到任何价值。政客们说，我们必须引领5G竞赛，但是他们从来没有具体地带回家赢得比赛对改善美国人意味着什么。生活。
 正如我们在测试中显示的那样，Verizon和AT＆amp; T的全国范围内5G的性能通常都比其4G差。 T-Mobile的中频带5G速度比其4G快，但仍在加拿大贝尔和Telus网络等优质4G网络范围内。
 在美国，唯一显示出真正新体验的5G类型是毫米波，通常被称为Verizon的UWB。即使经过两年的网络建设，最多也只有百分之几的美国人能够实现这一目标，而且大多数消费者还没有看到任何现实生活中的毫米波应用程序。 
（顺便说一下，其中一些原因是由于冠状病毒引起的。具有讽刺意味的是，少数具有早期潜力的mmWave应用程序之一使成千上万的人能够从拥挤的体育场或音乐厅流式传输高质量的视频，而不会阻塞手机。但是什么时候您最后一次进入其中一个？）
 多年来，我们一直在听5G将如何改变教育，行业，游戏……一切。这些都没有实现。一点儿都没有！将来可能会这样做，但是无线运营商在这一市场营销上举足轻重，在5G承诺和5G现实之间留有巨大差距。
 这就是阴谋理论可以应用的地方。承运人为此花费了很多钱。政客们似乎真的想要它。看起来很重要；到目前为止，它几乎没有带来任何优势。所以必须有一个秘密的议程，对吧？真正的背后是谁？为什么要花这么多钱？
  愚蠢，愚蠢的真正答案是阿吉特·派（Ajit Pai）的FCC搞砸了其频谱分配计划，最初选择了一个胆小鬼的方法，即不面对国防部或卫星公司清除5G最佳频率，尽管有巨大的政治压力要显示美国为“领导者”。在任何＆＃34;种族＆＃34;反对中国。
 政府和运营商还大大低估了构建28GHz网络的后勤难度，而新手机变得如此无聊，以至于运营商需要一个流行语来让您在整个美国非常糟糕的一年中进行升级。
    如果纳什维尔的轰炸机将5G视为一种监视工具，而我不是说他这样做了，那只是FBI的2020年版本，无线电就让我大吃一惊。这是经典的偏执狂。
 ＆＃34; 5G＆＃34;的概念已完全暗示自己已进入互联网的监视偏执狂社区。多年来，偏执狂妄想症的人们在专门针对目标人群的网站上相互分享和强化。这些网站之一现在在首页顶部显示了一个很大的5G图形。 
但是就像在后J时期反联邦调查局的妄想狂盛行一样。埃德加·胡佛（Edgar Hoover）时代原来是联邦调查局（FBI）将大量文件保存在里面，在这颗扭曲的珍珠中有一粒沙，这就是为什么如此多的错觉专门针对5G的原因。
 这并不是说5G带来了任何邪恶的事情。事实证明，与5G相比，消费者实际上无法做的事情很多，这与营销支出，政治辩论和国际性的hair头行为大相径庭。这不应该被忽略。这应该是一个教训– FCC，国会，无线运营商和无线行业需要向普通美国人展示一些真正的5G优势，或者暂时关闭它。

     The FBI is investigating whether Anthony Quinn Warner, the presumed Nashville suicide bomber, targeted a nearby AT&T building because of  paranoia over 5G-related government surveillance, according to several news reports.
 If so, that brings the anti-5G conspiracy nonsense to a new, even more destructive level. For several months now, idiots have been  targeting what they presume to be 5G towers (even  when they aren't) out of YouTube-soaked fears that 5G technology is harmful to their health. It's even reached my New York City neighborhood; across the street from my apartment there's graffiti that says "5G Kills Life."
 Let me be clear:  5G is not dangerous to your health, and it isn't being used to surveil you in a way that's any different from the 4G technology in your current smartphone. If you've spent any time watching a police procedural in the past decade, you know law enforcement can get warrants to track your phone-based location history or see who you've called or texted.  The New York Times  has shown how even anonymized location data can be used to track you using Wi-Fi, 3G, or 4G. There's nothing new to see there with 5G.
 Much of the anti-5G movement is a social-media-fueled, malicious grift. But Warner's 5G-surveillance fears, if true, open up a new front that shows a true weakness of 5G technology, 5G debate, and 5G messaging in the US.
    Health-related 5G conspiracies are often based in the idea that 5G is an untested technology that requires new infrastructure and is related to dangerous technologies like military anti-personnel weapons. In my mind, they build on pseudoscientists trying to make a dollar or a career out of fear, and potentially on  international bad actors trying to handicap rivals.
 Those conspiracy theories  aren't true on a  whole bunch of levels. But I think one reason why the wireless carriers are doing such a poor job counteracting these ideas is that they're afraid to reveal how mundane their 5G systems really are.
 If asked upfront they'll be honest: "nationwide" 5G right now offers the same performance as 4G ... because it's still dependent on 4G, runs on the same frequencies, and uses many of the same technologies. But they're not about to put that on billboards or promote it to investors. That wouldn't sell the latest phones from Apple or Samsung or get carriers' subscribers to upscale their service plans.
 The fact that 5G right now, for most Americans, is almost exactly like 4G, is the most powerful argument against conspiracy theories. But companies can't say that because it would pierce their marketing. They'd have to admit that when they said " 5G just got real," they jumped the gun.
 On a political level, meanwhile, 5G has become an incoherent hot button, used as an  incomprehensible buzzword by President Trump and thrown around Congress as a totem for an intangible technology race against China.
  I believe in the promise of 5G as a technology. I've read the specs. If implemented properly, with broad ecosystem support, it can do everything it promises. But it isn't doing any of it yet, billboards and presidential tweets notwithstanding. That then opens up the door, in a twisted mind, to the paranoia Warner may have believed.
   Wireless carriers and tech companies are all saying 5G is a huge, world-changing technology that's available now, but few Americans are seeing any value from it. Politicians are saying we must be in the lead in the "race to 5G," but they never bring home concretely what winning that race means for improving Americans' lives.
 As we've shown in our tests, both  Verizon's and  AT&T's nationwide 5G often offer weaker performance than their 4G. T-Mobile's mid-band 5G is faster than its 4G, but still well in the range of good 4G networks like the  Bell and Telus networks in Canada.
 The only type of 5G in the US that  shows a truly new experience is millimeter-wave, most commonly known as Verizon's "UWB." Even after two years of network-building, only a  single-digit percentage of Americans at most can get that, and most consumers haven't seen any real-life millimeter-wave applications.
 (Some of that, by the way, is ironically because of coronavirus. One of the few great, early potential mmWave applications lets thousands of people stream high-quality video from a crowded stadium or concert hall without their phones getting choked up ... but when's the last time you were in one of those?)
 We've been hearing for years about how 5G will change education, industry, gaming ... everything. None of that has come to pass. Not even a little bit! It may do so in the future, but the wireless carriers seriously jumped the gun on this marketing, leaving a huge gap between 5G promises and 5G reality.
 And that's where conspiracy theories can jump in. The carriers are spending a ton of money on this; politicians really seem to want it; it seems very important; it has so far brought you little or no advantage. So there must be a secret agenda, right? Who's really behind this? Why is so much money being spent?
  The dumb, dull, true answer is that Ajit Pai's FCC  screwed up its spectrum allocation plan, initially choosing a coward's way out of not facing down the DoD or satellite companies to clear out the best frequencies for 5G, despite massive political pressures to show the US as a "leader" in any "race" against China.
 The government and the carriers also wildly underestimated the logistical difficulty of building 28GHz networks, and new phones have become so boring that carriers need a buzzword to get you to upgrade during an overall very bad year for America in general.
    If the Nashville bomber saw 5G as a surveillance tool—and I'm not saying he did—yes, that's just a 2020 version of "the FBI put radios in my teeth." It's classic paranoia.
 The concept of "5G" has insinuated itself thoroughly into the internet's surveillance-paranoia communities. For years now, people with paranoid delusions have shared and reinforced them with each other on websites dedicated to "targeted individuals." One of those sites now shows a  big 5G graphic, right at the top of the homepage.
 But just as anti-FBI paranoia thrived during the post-J. Edgar Hoover era when it turned out the FBI was  keeping files on a huge number of people, there's a grain of sand in this twisted pearl, a reason why so many of these delusions have locked on to 5G specifically.
 That grain isn't that anything evil is being done with 5G. It's that nothing much is being done with 5G that consumers can actually perceive, massively out of proportion to the marketing spend, political debate, and international hair-tearing it has incurred. And that shouldn't be ignored. It should be a lesson—that the FCC, Congress, wireless carriers, and the wireless industry need to show some real 5G benefits for the average American, or shut up about it for a while.

title: 纳什维尔轰炸机报告的偏执狂：5G现实与营销之间
title_en: Nashville Bomber's Reported Paranoia: Between 5G Reality and Marketing

Janine Soleil是60年代的孩子，自称为嬉皮，于5月14日在纽约皇后区死于COVID-19。她75岁。她从不拥抱现代技术，直到几年前才寄给她最好的朋友在打字机上打字的信。当她于五月去世时，她的儿子画家Dylan Gauthier着手进行一项有关她的数字数据遗产的多媒体艺术项目，以寻找甚至拒绝互联网的人在电子数据库中留下的东西。
 Soleil于1945年出生于布鲁克林，曾在UCLA学习芭蕾舞，并在旧金山的Haight-Ashbury区度过了一段时间，之后成为加利福尼亚州奇诺市以及后来的加利福尼亚州昆西小镇的社会工作者和家庭治疗师。与高迪耶（Gauthier）的父亲离婚后，她将姓氏改成了Soleil（她选择了法语，是因为她喜欢这种声音）。
 作为60年代和70年代后期在加利福尼亚州的一名年轻女子，Soleil参与了针对环境和女权主义事业的积极行动。她的最好的朋友莉娜·奥斯特罗夫（Lena Ostroff）于1969年与Soleil会面，当时他们俩都在一个名为洛杉矶妇女中心的女权组织工作。两位女士开始了长达数十年的友谊，Soleil在世界各地的不同地方拜访了Ostroff。 “我们立即感到自己是同志。”
    索雷尔（Soleil）年龄大了几岁，并将奥斯特洛夫（Ostroff）引入了加利福尼亚的一些新潮和新潮事物中-有些像糙米一样简单，还有其他诸如奥斯特洛夫（Ostroff）儿子出生后的母乳喂养支持组织La Leche League。 “她对我影响很大，”奥斯特罗夫（Ostroff）告诉BuzzFeed新闻。 “她是我的购物大师。我们曾经一起逛街，她告诉我，我在挑选“老太太的衣服”。
 作为治疗师和社会工作者，Soleil的一生都在传播善良并帮助他人。 “她谈到了海星理论：您在海滩上看到一个海星，然后捡起一个海星，看着它，然后说，‘您好吗，海星？好的，我要把你放回大海，’”高迪耶对BuzzFeed新闻说。 “这就是她赋予生活意义，帮助人们，聆听他们的故事，与他们一起大笑的意义。”
 她的长期朋友和伴侣Geoffrey Quinsey在90年代后期通过在线约会网站认识了她。她的个人资料跳到他身上。 “她不是你的普通人。当时我无法确切地告诉她她不是平均水平。”他对BuzzFeed新闻说。从事IT工作的Quinsey更加注重实际。 “我立即担心这个花童可能不适合我。”他错了，两人之间的爱情和友谊持续了20多年。 “我们总是很奇怪。她一直是个“搞砸灯泡”的人。她对事物不感兴趣，对人不感兴趣。”在Soleil于70年代初被诊断出患有痴呆症之后，Quinsey和Ostroff帮助她从加利福尼亚搬到皇后区的记忆保健机构，以使其与儿子及其家人更近。昆西现在有她的狗格蕾丝。
    母亲去世后，高蒂埃（Gauthier）构思了《当我消失时删除我：降低在线数据实体的道德框架》，一项艺术研究项目和多媒体工作，涉及一个人（甚至是拒绝数字的人）留下的数据技术-死亡。该项目由数字艺术组织Eyebeam资助，作为其针对COVID-19的“快速响应”计划的一部分。 
Gauthier的多媒体项目将跟踪她的数据，并最终将其整合到雕塑作品中，其中可能还包括一些骨灰。它还将有一个应用程序，人们可以在该应用程序中找到有关数据死亡后发生的情况的信息。也将有一个部分与当地政府联系，以请求或倡导有关用户数据隐私的法律和政策。
 像Facebook这样的社交媒体平台-以及不久之后的Twitter-都有纪念帐户的步骤，人们甚至开始计划命名数字执行者去世后处理其电子邮件和社交帐户之类的事情。但是Gauthier指出，这与您制作的内容有关-您的电子邮件或tweet，而不是诸如购买历史记录或广告定位数据之类的无源数据。 Gauthier表示：“即使人们制定了更加自觉的计划，但对于我们不一定需要访问的数据以及公司在其上汇总，增加，出售和创建数据库条目的数据，也无法做很多事情。” “这与在COVID周围发生的死亡数量相乘-以及围绕联系跟踪和医疗数据的数据加速-这些是处理损失和悲剧的人们所没有想到的事情。”
 他说：“在某人的来世事务中成为一名执行人，使我了解到像她这样的人在网上会有多少残留的人，以及在那里存在多少关于她的数据。” “这让我思考了我们有意并打算在世界上离开的事物与我们不存在的事物，并考虑将其可视化为冰山一角。”
 “我妈妈就是这个嬉皮士Luddite，” Gauthier说。 “我有点把Luddism看作是一种与精神背道而驰的特定心理状态-当Bob Dylan拿起电吉他时发疯的人。我妈妈把自己装扮成拒绝技术的人。”
 但是尽管她可能拒绝了它，但是她无法逃脱它。 Soleil拥有电话，拥有一所房屋，她用信用卡购买了商品并在网上购物，所有这些都为数据经纪人留下了痕迹。 （这些公司买卖您的数据，包括TransUnion之类的信用报告机构，Spokeo之类的网站出售您的地址或电话号码之类的信息，或者通过Oracle或Acxiom等大公司出售匿名广告定位数据。可以选择退出这些大数据经纪人，但这并不容易。）
 “作为犹太佛教徒，她不想留下会在地球上留下足迹的痕迹，＆＃34;高迪耶说。 “但是收集到的关于她的所有这些数据都存储在数据场中，可以买卖。”
 Soleil的儿子，her妇Kendra Sullivan和孙子Demetri以及哥哥Barry Beiderman，大家庭和亲爱的朋友得以幸存。据她的儿子说，她要求在将来的某个时候举行一场舞会。在Zoom举行了纪念活动。
note_en:
Janine Soleil, a child of the ’60s and self-described hippie, died of  COVID-19 on May 14 in Queens, New York. She was 75. Never embracing modern technology, she sent her best friend letters typed on a typewriter up until a few years ago. When she died in May, her son, artist Dylan Gauthier, embarked on a multimedia art project about her digital data legacy, searching out what even someone who rejected the internet had left behind in electronic databases.
 Born in Brooklyn in 1945, Soleil studied ballet at UCLA and spent time in the Haight-Ashbury district in San Francisco, before becoming a social worker and family therapist in Chino, California, and later the nearby small town of Quincy, California. After divorcing Gauthier’s father, she changed her last name to Soleil — a French word she picked because she liked the sound.
 As a young woman in California in the late ’60s and ’70s, Soleil was involved in progressive activism for environmental and feminist causes. Her best friend, Lena Ostroff, met Soleil in 1969 when they both worked at a feminist organization called the Los Angeles Women’s Center. The two women began a friendship that spanned decades, with Soleil visiting Ostroff in various places where she lived around the world. “We immediately felt like kindred spirits.”
    Soleil was a few years older and introduced Ostroff to some of the new and trendy things in California — some as simple as brown rice, and others like the breastfeeding support group  La Leche League after Ostroff’s son was born. “She had a great influence on me,” Ostroff told BuzzFeed News. “She was my shopping guru. We used to shop together and she’d tell me I was picking out ‘old lady clothes.’”
 As a therapist and social worker, Soleil’s life’s work was spreading kindness and helping others. “She talked about the starfish theory: You see a starfish on the beach, and you pick up a starfish and look at it and say, ‘Are you OK, starfish? OK, I'm going to put you back in the ocean,’” Gauthier told BuzzFeed News. “This is what gave her meaning in life, helping people, hearing their stories, laughing with them.”
 Geoffrey Quinsey, her longtime friend and partner, met her through an online dating site in the late ’90s. Her profile jumped out to him. “She wasn’t your average person. At that time I wasn’t able to tell exactly in what way she wasn’t average,” he told BuzzFeed News. Quinsey, who worked in IT, was more practical-minded. “I was immediately concerned that this flower child was perhaps not suited for me.” He was wrong — the two found a love and friendship that lasted over 20 years. “We were always an odd mix. She was always a ‘can't screw in a lightbulb’ person. She wasn't interested in things, she was interested in people.” After Soleil was diagnosed with dementia in her early seventies, Quinsey and Ostroff helped her move from California to a memory care facility in Queens to be closer to her son and his family. Quinsey now has her dog, Gracie.
    After his mother’s death, Gauthier conceived of   Delete Me When I’m Gone: An Ethical Framework for Drawing Down Our Online Data Bodies, an artistic research project and multimedia work about the data that is left behind when a person — even someone who rejected digital technology — dies. The project is funded by the digital arts organization Eyebeam, as part of their “Rapid Response”  program in response to COVID-19.
 Gauthier’s multimedia project that will track her data and eventually incorporate it into a sculptural work, possibly including some of her ashes. It will also have an app where people can find out information about what happens to their data after they die. There will also be a section for contacting local governments to request or advocate for laws and policies around user data privacy.
 Social media platforms like Facebook — and soon, Twitter — have steps for memorializing an account, and people are even starting to make plans for naming digital executors to handle things like their email and social accounts after they die. But Gauthier pointed out that this is about the content you make — your emails or tweets, not the kind of passive data that is floating out there like purchase history or your ad targeting data. “Even while people are making those more conscious plans, there’s not a lot that can be done about the data that we don’t necessarily have access to and companies aggregate, multiply, sell, and create database entries on,” said Gauthier. “That, multiplied with the amount of death happening around COVID — and the acceleration of data around contact tracking and medical data — these are things that people dealing with loss and tragedy are not thinking about.”
 “Becoming an executor as someone’s afterlife affairs exposed me to how much someone like her would have a residual presence online and how much data about her existed out there,” he said. “It made me think about the things we are intentionally and planning to leave in the world versus the things we are not, and thinking about visualizing this as the tip of the iceberg.”
 “My mom was this total hippie Luddite,” said Gauthier. “I kind of think of that Luddism as this specific mental state that goes against the grain — someone who’s mad when Bob Dylan picks up an electric guitar. My mom framed herself as someone who rejected technology.”
 But while she may have rejected it, she could not escape it. Soleil had a phone and she owned a house, she bought things with credit cards and shopped online, all things that leave a breadcrumb trail for  data brokers. (These are companies that buy and sell your data, including from credit reporting bureaus like TransUnion, sites like Spokeo that sell information like your address or phone number, or anonymized ad targeting data through big firms like Oracle or Acxiom. It’s possible to  opt out of these big data brokers, but it’s not easy.)
 “As a Jewish Buddist person, she didn’t want to leave a trace that would leave a footprint on the planet," said Gauthier. “But all this data that was collected about her that is being stored in data farms, being bought and sold.”
 Soleil is survived by her son, her daughter-in-law, Kendra Sullivan, and grandson Demetri, as well as her brother Barry Beiderman, extended family, and dear friends. According to her son, she requested a party with lots of dancing sometime in the future. A memorial was held over Zoom.
title: 当他的母亲去世于Covid-19时，这位艺术家开始追踪她的在线数据
title_en: When His Mother Died of Covid-19, This Artist Started Tracking Her Online Data

炸药早已不复存在。他们最后一具带壳的，多触角的物种消失了6600万年前，这是有史以来最严重的物种灭绝之一。撞击地球并在白垩纪上拉开帷幕的近10公里宽的小行星将它们消灭了，就像飞行的翼龙和非禽类恐龙一样。
 然而，仍有幸存者。尽管有70％以上的已知物种在灾难中灭绝，但还有许多其他物种得以幸存。现在，古生物学家面临的难题是：为什么多产且长寿的亚mon虫会灭亡，而其他海洋生物（包括他们的远房表亲，鹦鹉螺和鱿鱼）却会持续存在？毕竟，炸药以前在其他三个物种大灭绝中幸免于难。
 看看是什么使白垩纪末日如此灾难性，提供了一些线索。头足类动物以前经历的大规模灭绝是对地球系统的逐渐扰动，例如数十万年的剧烈火山活动。但是，当小行星在白垩纪末期进入行星时，其影响是空前的。在第一天，地球大气层加热到类似烤箱的温度。不久之后，撞击产生的碎屑以及喷射器引发的大面积森林大火产生的烟灰开始遮挡阳光。光合作用停止了多年，导致生态系统几乎完全崩溃。
 海洋中的生命遭受了痛苦。大多数海洋生态系统都依赖光合作用，但漫长的夜晚消灭了无数这些多产的自养生物，几乎使海洋恢复了一种细菌状态，这种状态已经有十亿年了。生存下来的人都必须吃其他生物，才能在黑暗中挣扎。
 对于那些通常靠自己的后代喂养的炸药来说，这可能已经证明是一个问题。
 小行星的撞击也将碳释放到大气中，使海洋变成酸性，这种情况会使婴儿的浮游性铵盐难以形成贝壳。纽约自然历史博物馆的古生物学家阿曼·塔吉卡（Amane Tajika）说，如果他们丧生，那么任何幸存的亚am人都会被剥夺这种重要的食物来源。
 在突然结束之前，炸药正在蓬勃发展。到白垩纪末期，许多铵盐专门化为特定的生态位-在海洋中占据着不同的深度-并且需要相对更多的能量才能生长。炸药可能已经被用来满足海洋资源的需求，以支持其快速生长贝壳的需求，因此当这些资源变得稀缺时，遭受了痛苦。他们的表兄弟鹦鹉螺取而代之的是缓慢发展的通才路线。 
而且，就像灭绝期间经常发生的那样，运气可能助长了亚mon人的灭亡。
 犹他大学的古生物学家凯瑟琳·里特布什（Kathleen Ritterbush）说：“现代鱿鱼及其亲属在季节，年度和年代际尺度上都有令人讨厌的繁荣与萧条周期。”尽管科学家和捕捞业记录了数据，但没人知道是什么导致这些通量。 “我们不能说他们下周二将要做什么。”
 炸药也可能会跟随这种不稳定的周期。 Ritterbush说，如果小行星在炸药爆炸时撞上小行星，“您可以在最糟糕的一天给最小的炸药种群加油。”
 即使古生物学家能够束缚潜水装备并潜入白垩纪晚期海洋，仍然很难找到am石消失的原因。在事实发生六千六百万年之后再做这项工作将更具挑战性。但是这个故事不仅对理解损失很重要。白垩纪末期发生的一切为我们现代海洋奠定了基础。众所周知，亚。人的终结标志着海洋的开始。

note_en:
Ammonites are long gone. The last of their coil-shelled, many-tentacled kind disappeared 66 million years ago in one of the worst mass extinctions of all time. The nearly 10-kilometer-wide asteroid that struck the Earth and drew the curtain on the Cretaceous wiped them out, just as it did the flying pterosaurs and non-avian dinosaurs.
 And yet, there were survivors. While more than 70 percent of known species went extinct during the disaster, many others survived. The puzzle facing paleontologists now: why did the prolific and long-lived ammonites perish while other marine life—including their distant cousins, the nautilus and squid—persist? After all, ammonites had previously survived three other mass extinctions.
 Looking at what made the end of the Cretaceous so catastrophic provides some clues. The previous mass extinctions the cephalopods had managed to weather were gradual perturbations of Earth’s systems, like intense volcanic activity that played out over hundreds of thousands of years. But when the asteroid whapped into the planet at the end of the Cretaceous, the effects were unprecedented. Within the first day, the Earth’s atmosphere heated to oven-like temperatures. Soon after, debris from the impact, and soot from widespread forest fires sparked by the ejecta, started to blot out the sun. Photosynthesis stopped for years, causing a near total collapse of the ecosystem.
 Life in the ocean suffered. Most ocean ecosystems rely on photosynthesizers, but the extended night wiped out countless of these prolific autotrophs, nearly reverting the ocean to a bacterial state not seen for a billion years. Whatever survived had to eat other organisms to make it through the dark.
 That may have proved a problem for ammonites, which routinely fed on their own offspring.
 The asteroid’s impact also released carbon into the atmosphere, turning the ocean acidic—a condition that would have made it harder for baby planktonic ammonites to form shells. If they perished, any surviving ammonite adults would have been deprived of this critical food source, says Amane Tajika, a paleontologist at the American Museum of Natural History in New York.
 Before their sudden end, ammonites were flourishing. By the end of the Cretaceous many ammonites had specialized to particular niches—occupying  varied depths within the seas—and required comparatively  more energy to grow. Ammonites might have been used to ocean resources supporting their needs to rapidly grow shells, and so suffered when those resources became scarce. Their cousin the nautilus instead took the slow-growing, generalist route.
 And, as often happens during extinctions, bad luck may have had a hand in the ammonites’ demise.
 “Modern squid and their relatives have obscene boom-bust cycles on seasonal, annual, and decadal timescales,” says Kathleen Ritterbush, a paleontologist at the University of Utah. Despite data recorded by scientists and the fishing industry, no one knows what causes these fluxes. “We can’t say what they’re going to do next Tuesday.”
 It’s possible that ammonites also followed such volatile cycles. If the asteroid struck during an ammonite bust, “you could tank the smallest ammonite population on their worst day,” Ritterbush says.
 Even if paleontologists were able to strap on scuba gear and dive into the Late Cretaceous ocean, finding the reason why ammonites disappeared would still be difficult. Doing it 66 million years after the fact is infinitely more challenging. But the story isn’t just important for understanding a loss. What transpired at the end of the Cretaceous set the foundation of our modern ocean. The end of the ammonites marks the beginning of the ocean as we know it.

公众和新闻界只是从SEC方面听到了这个故事，我们将在几周内提出回应，以解决针对Ripple的未经证实的指控。
 SEC决定采取这一行动的决定不仅与Ripple有关，而且是对美国整个加密产业的攻击。我们一直说过，美国加密货币监管缺乏明确的危险，因为他们的诉讼已经影响了无数无辜的XRP零售持有者，与Ripple无关。它还不必要地使交易所，做市商和交易者的水浑身泥泞。 SEC已将更多不确定因素引入市场，从而积极损害了他们本应保护的社区。因此，一些市场参与者对此采取保守的态度也就不足为奇了。
 同时，Ripple将继续为美国和全球的所有产品和客户提供运营和支持。我们的大多数客户都不在美国，而且XRP的总体交易量主要在美国境外进行。例如，在英国，日本，瑞士和新加坡使用XRP的路途很明确。八年来，我们已经开发出可帮助数百名客户解决全球支付难题的产品-我们将为我们的公司辩护，并期待着在法庭上解决此问题，以最终使美国加密货币行业更加清晰。
 同时，我们也期待与所有专员和SEC新任命的领导层一起工作。总体而言，SEC主席，每个SEC部门的六名董事，SEC的首席经济学家和SEC的总法律顾问现已离任（许多人刚于上周离开）。我们对建设性监管参与的坚定承诺没有改变。
note_en:
The public and press have only heard the story from the SEC’s side, and we’ll be filing our response in a few weeks to address these unproven allegations against Ripple.
 The SEC’s decision to file this action is not just about Ripple, it is an attack on the entire crypto industry here in the United States. We’ve always said that there is a dangerous lack of regulatory clarity for crypto in the U.S. — their lawsuit has already affected countless innocent XRP retail holders with no connection to Ripple. It has also needlessly muddied the waters for exchanges, market makers and traders. The SEC has introduced more uncertainty into the market, actively harming the community they’re supposed to protect. It’s no surprise that some market participants are reacting conservatively as a result.
 In the meantime, Ripple will continue to operate and support all products and customers in the U.S. and globally. The majority of our customers aren’t in the U.S. and overall XRP volume is largely traded outside of the U.S. There are clear rules of the road for using XRP in the UK, Japan, Switzerland and Singapore, for example. For eight years, we’ve built products that help hundreds of customers solve pain points around global payments — we will defend our company and look forward to settling this matter in court to finally get clarity for the U.S. crypto industry.
 On a parallel note, we also look forward to working with all of the Commissioners and the SEC’s new leadership, once appointed. In all, the SEC Chair, six of his Directors from each SEC Division, the SEC’s Chief Economist and the SEC’s General Counsel have now departed (many left just last week). Our steadfast commitment to constructive regulatory engagement has not changed.
